Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking gray shapes near bars also only changed timer. Bars at minimum (5 pixels). I've used 16 of ~20 actions. Need to try remaining elements: the other two gray shapes (id=11, id=21) near containers, or the bars themselves.
- Goal model: Completely consume all bars by clicking matching shapes in containers.
- Action model: Need to find what actually changes the board. The cross patterns, containers, or colored shapes inside containers might be the interactive elements.
- Recent findings: Clicking on the red dot at (10, 31) caused a board change. I need to see what exactly changed.
- Plan: Try clicking the gray shapes near containers (id=11 at (19,42), id=21 at (41,22)).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Still only the timer bar changed. I've now tried clicking:
1. Shapes inside containers → decreases bars (this worked)
2. Isolated red dots → only timer
3. Cross centers → only timer
4. Gray shapes near bars (id=1, id=15) → only timer
5. Gray shapes near containers (id=11, id=21) → only timer
I'm at 18 of ~20 actions. I need to figure this out quickly. Let me think about what I haven't tried:
- The bars themselves (green bar at ~row 9-11, col 28-29; yellow bar at ~row 28-29, col 9-10)
- The container frames themselves (gray outer frames)
- The dark gray inner frames
- The red cross arms (not centers)
Let me try clicking on the bars themselves. The green bar (N color) and yellow bar (Y color).
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem. The bars started at some size and I reduced them to 5 pixels. Maybe the goal isn't to reduce them to 0 but to match some target. Or maybe I need to click on the bars to "submit" them.
Let me also consider that maybe the level completion requires clicking on ALL the interactive elements, not just reducing the bars. Let me check if there's a pattern.
Actually, I should try clicking on the bars themselves. Let me look at their boundaries:
- Green bar (N, id=2): boundary [[9, 28], [9, 29], [11, 29]] - 5 pixels
- Yellow bar (Y, id=16): boundary [[28, 9], [29, 10], [28, 11]] - 5 pixels
Let me try clicking on these bars.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: All non-shape clicks only change timer. I'm at 18/20 actions. Only remaining untested elements are the bars themselves and container frames. Let me try clicking the bars directly.
Plan: Click on the green bar and yellow bar themselves.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the bars themselves
# Green bar (N) at around (9, 28)
# Yellow bar (Y) at around (28, 9)
actions = [
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 9, 'col': 28},   # green bar
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 28, 'col': 9},   # yellow bar
]
result = action(actions)
print(f"Result: {result}")

# Check what changed
if last_transition:
    before = last_transition.before_frame
    after = last_transition.after_frame
    before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before.segmentation['nodes']}
    after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after.segmentation['nodes']}
    
    print("\nChanges:")
    for nid in sorted(set(list(before_nodes.keys()) + list(after_nodes.keys()))):
        bn = before_nodes.get(nid)
        an = after_nodes.get(nid)
        if bn is None:
            print(f"  NEW id={nid}, color={an['color']}, pixels={an['pixels']}")
        elif an is None:
            print(f"  REMOVED id={nid}, color={bn['color']}, pixels={bn['pixels']}")
        elif bn['pixels'] != an['pixels']:
            print(f"  CHANGED id={nid}, color={bn['color']}, pixels={bn['pixels']}->{an['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
